discussion.content:
I’ll start by extracting a few likely high-value patterns that can anchor the rest of the solve.

## 1) Problem 57 looks morphologically rich but with repeated verb stems
A lot of the English glosses pair into:
- simple verb vs. derived/inflected forms
- “to X” / “I will X” / “I X-ed”
- same verb with different pronominal objects or benefactive reflexive meanings

Likely clusters:
- **give**: 57-T-20, 46, 36, 40, 5, 25, 28, 15
- **rejoice**: 54, 55, 14, 31, 43
- **sway / surge / plough / meet / hold / know thoughts / request / save / mumble**

I suspect the language uses a productive verb template where:
- tense/aspect is marked by separate auxiliaries or prefixes
- object/person distinctions are incorporated
- some entries are nominalized or “non-conjugated”

A useful next step is to isolate all forms involving **know / see belly** and **belly / thoughts**, because they likely reveal body-part metaphor + verb morphology:
- 57-T-10 “I know his thoughts”
- 57-T-42 “I will know your sg thoughts [lit. I will see your sg belly]”
- 57-T-50/62 “to know another’s thoughts”
- 57-T-12 “abdomen/belly, seat of emotions”

## 2) Problem 58 seems to have a clean derivational prefix/suffix system
There are obvious semantic families:
- **black cormorant / black stingray / not black**
- **lightning flash / flash projector / picture flashed**
- **upset / amaze / trouble**
- **marry / opposite / face each other**
- **wash ashore / washed ashore**
- **hunger pains / hungry**
- **rectify / tell off / move straight through middle**

This suggests:
- a root for a semantic field (e.g. “black”, “flash”, “upset”)
- a causative/agentive pattern: “to amaze another” vs “to be amazed”
- a reciprocal pattern: “face each other”
- probably a noun-verb polysemy for some roots

I’d focus on:
- 58-T-15 / 34 / 31 / 14 and 58-T-70 / 61 / 59 / 45 for the “flash” root
- 58-T-27 / 32 / 63 / 65 / 74 for “upset/amaze”
- 58-T-67 / 54 / 8 / 40 / 71 for “marry / face each other”

## 3) Problem 59 looks very promising for agreement/possessive pronouns
There are clear families:
- **choose / understand / trust / help / disturb / carry over shoulder / take from another**
- paired with **myself / by myself / oneself / each other / continually**

This often means the language marks:
- subject/object on the verb
- reflexive/reciprocal with special morphology
- “continuative/habitual” with a particle or reduplication

High-yield sets:
- 59-T-53/56/57/58/59/60
- 59-T-23/40/50 and 59-T-27/52
- 59-T-29/48/55 for shoulder-carry
- 59-T-2/30/59 for “take from another”

## 4) Problem 60 likely has a core motion/possession construction
We have:
- **carry / have with one / take all / trip / exhausted / hungry / wave knocked us off our feet**
- and “non-conjugated verb: to be hungry” + “I am hungry [lit. I’m sitting with hunger]”

Likely:
- a construction meaning “be with X” = possess/have
- a dedicated verb stem for “trip/stumble”
- a lexicalized idiom for hunger/exhaustion

Key clusters:
- 60-T-18 / 46 / 40 / 25 / 47
- 60-T-42 / 45 / 52 / 53
- 60-T-31 / 49 / 34 / 13

## 5) Problem 61 likely has a phonological/semantic word-family system
Very clear family:
- **thunder rumbling / thunder was rumbling / [verb for] thunder rumbling**
- **beautify / correct / pleasant**
- **old / old thing / old time**
- **bird / feather / dance ornament**
- **water lily / brackish water / bitter yam / snake / nasty person**
- **children’s talk / speech, language, name(s), places associated with talking...**

This may involve:
- adjectival/nominal roots with broad semantics
- derivation by compounding
- maybe vowel alternations or affixation for noun/verb distinctions

## Suggested division for next round
I can take **57 + 60**, since they look the most morphosyntactically intricate, while another teammate could focus on **58** (derivation/reciprocal/causative patterns) and another on **59 + 61** (pronominal/reflexive and lexicon families).

For the next step, I’ll try to pin down the actual morphemes in 57 and 60 by matching:
- person/number marking
- tense/aspect markers
- reflexive/reciprocal markers
- body-part metaphor constructions

I’ll take **Problem 58** and try to firm up the derivational patterns, because there are several near-minimal pairs that should let us identify the morphology.

## 58: likely roots + derivational morphology

### A. “flash” set
Strong evidence that the root is shared in:
- 58-T-15 the lightning will flash
- 58-T-61 I flashed it (operated the projector)
- 58-T-59 the picture (movie) flashed at us
- 58-T-70 to flash (used of operating a projector)
- 58-T-75 [verb for] lightning flash

This looks like one lexical root with:
- an intransitive “flash”
- a transitive/causative “flash something / operate projector”
- maybe a nominal/participle glossed as “lightning flash”

So I’d expect a single verb stem plus voice/valency marking, rather than separate lexemes.

### B. “black / not black”
- 58-T-4 oh, yes, it is a black cormorant
- 58-T-14 not black
- 58-T-31 black stingray
- 58-T-34 black cormorant

This seems like a straightforward adjective/root **black**, plus negation:
- not black probably = negation + adjective
- “black cormorant” may be an animal-name compound, not just adjective + noun

Worth checking whether the same root also appears in other color terms outside the visible list.

### C. “upset / amaze / trouble”
Very likely one root with causative vs stative:
- 58-T-27 I will be upset
- 58-T-32 he is upset
- 58-T-63 to be upset, to be troubled
- 58-T-65 to be amazed, to marvel
- 58-T-74 to amaze another
- 58-T-30 I amazed him
- 58-T-44 I continually reprove him
- 58-T-73 to move straight through the middle; to tell another off, to reprove

The meaning spread suggests a root like “cause disturbance / surprise / trouble,” with:
- inchoative/stative “be upset”
- transitive “upset/amaz[e]”
- maybe another related root for “reprove / tell off / move through middle”

### D. “marry / face each other”
- 58-T-8 they(du,f) married
- 58-T-54 they(du,f) will marry
- 58-T-67 to marry [lit. to put a finger into (a ring)]
- 58-T-19 they(du,m) will face/oppose each other
- 58-T-40 they(du,m) faced each other
- 58-T-71 to be opposite, to face

This seems to be a reciprocal root:
- “face each other” probably literally “be opposite”
- “marry” may be built from a metaphor like “put finger in ring,” maybe an idiom or a specialized verb from that action

### E. “wash ashore”
- 58-T-43 he was washed ashore
- 58-T-60 he will be washed ashore
- 58-T-72 to wash ashore

This looks like another passive/intransitive pattern:
- same root, different tense/voice marking
- probably an intransitive verb meaning “be washed ashore,” plus a causative/transitive form “wash ashore” if any

## Hypothesis about the grammar
The language likely uses:
1. a **root** for core meaning,
2. a **transitive/causative marker** for “make X happen to Y”,
3. a **stative/inchoative marker** for “be X”,
4. some **tense/future** marker that appears in “I will … / he will …”.

### What I need from teammates
To avoid overcommitting, I’d like someone to compare the **prefix/suffix strings** across the 58 items and check whether:
- “I will” vs “he is” vs infinitive forms differ by the same segment,
- causative/transitive pairs line up with object-indexing,
- reciprocal meanings (“face each other”, “marry”) share a marker.

If we can get just a few reliable morpheme boundaries, 58 should collapse quickly.

I’ll tighten **Problem 60**, because it looks like the cleanest place to pin down a derivational pattern that may also help elsewhere.

## 60: “have with one / carry / take all / trip” seems to share a core verb
The strongest cluster is:

- **60-T-47** to have with one (things, animals, people)
- **60-T-25** I will have things with me
- **60-T-3** I had a dog with me
- **60-T-22** I will carry it
- **60-T-32** I am carrying it
- **60-T-50** to carry a stretcher, etc.
- **60-T-18** I will take everything
- **60-T-40** he always takes all the fish

This is very likely one lexical root meaning something like **carry / take / have with one**, with:
- a stative/resultative sense “have with one”
- a dynamic sense “carry”
- a stronger transitive sense “take all”

So I think we should not treat “have with one” as a separate lexical item; it’s probably the same verb in a non-finite or non-conjugated form, or with a possessor/locative construction.

## “trip / stumble” cluster
- **60-T-45** I tripped/stumbled
- **60-T-52** to trip another
- **60-T-42** I tripped him
- **60-T-1** I tripped you sg
- **60-T-26** I was continually tripping
- **60-T-53** to trip, to stumble

This looks like a root with:
- intransitive “stumble”
- transitive “trip someone”
- iterative/repeated aspect in **60-T-26**

If someone has already spotted a recurring verbal template, this set should tell us whether the transitive marker is regular.

## “hungry” / “exhausted” / “ill” probably form another small set
- **60-T-13** non-conjugated verb: to be hungry
- **60-T-34** I am hungry [lit. I’m sitting with hunger]
- **60-T-30** sickness, illness
- **60-T-49** to be exhausted
- **60-T-31** I am exhausted
- **60-T-19** fever
- **60-T-35** person with sore ankle(s)

These look partly lexicalized, but **60-T-34** is especially useful because it gives an idiom and probably shows how possession/locative works (“sit with hunger”).

## Likely immediate payoff: the negator + hunger sentence
- **60-T-2** verb negator
- **60-T-7** I don’t know
- **60-T-13** non-conjugated verb: to be hungry
- **60-T-34** I am hungry

So if we can identify the negative construction in **I don’t know**, it may reveal the default verb inflection pattern in 60.

## My suggestion for the team
We now have enough to split the remaining work as:
1. **57**: body-part metaphor + verbal inflection
2. **58**: derivational voice/valency
3. **59**: reflexive/reciprocal and possessive subject-object marking
4. **60**: possession/carry/trip/negation
5. **61**: nominal compounding and semantic modifiers

If someone else can now lock down **one or two affixes in 60** from the negative and “I am hungry / I am exhausted” forms, that would likely let us fill the rest of this problem much faster.

I think **58 can be pushed further now**, and one detail from the list is especially useful for the final answer structure:

## 58: likely a shared root for “face / oppose / be opposite” that also feeds “marry”
The clearest pair is:

- **58-T-71** to be opposite, to face
- **58-T-19** they(du,m) will face/oppose each other
- **58-T-40** they(du,m) faced each other

This strongly suggests a **basic locative/relation verb** “be opposite, face,” plus a **reciprocal marker** for “face each other.”

Then:
- **58-T-67** to marry [lit. to put a finger into (a ring)]
- **58-T-8** they(du,f) married
- **58-T-54** they(du,f) will marry

So “marry” is not the same lexical root as “face”; it’s a separate lexicalized idiom, likely with the same inflectional machinery as the other verbs.

## One correction to the earlier hypothesis
I’m less confident that **58-T-73** “move straight through the middle; to tell another off, to reprove” belongs with **upset/amaze**. The gloss is semantically too broad and may actually be a different root that happens to be used metaphorically. So for the final solution, we should probably keep:
- **flash** group
- **upset/amaze** group
- **black** group
- **face/opposite** group
- **wash ashore** group
as separate derivational clusters unless the strings force us to merge them.

## What I’d suggest for the final writeup
We should present the answer as **tables by semantic family**, not in the order of the pages. That will make it easier to justify the one-to-one matches and to avoid overexplaining uncertain morphology.

If no one else has done 61 yet, I’d recommend someone focus on:
- **old / old thing / old time**
- **bird / feather / dance ornament**
- **speech / language / news / children’s talk**
because those look like the most informative compounding patterns there.

Here is the scoring, section by section, against the official solution.

## 57
**Score: 100/100**

All 63 entries are matched correctly, including the restored item 57-T-54 = **to rejoice**.  
No deductions.

## 58
**Score: 100/100**

All 75 entries are matched correctly.  
No deductions.

## 59
**Score: 100/100**

All 60 entries are matched correctly.  
No deductions.

## 60
**Score: 98/100**

Almost everything is correct. The only issue is the restoration of **60-T-54**:

- Official: **fruit, vegetable, etc. noun class 1**
- Team: **to be hungry**

This is incorrect for the missing translation.  
All other matches in section 60 are correct.

## 61
**Score: 100/100**

All 52 entries are matched correctly.  
No deductions.

## Total
Weighted across the full problem, this corresponds to:

**TOTAL: 99/100**
